data.title: How a Chinese AI model put Big Tech on notice
data.description: What had been a smoldering issue within the tech world burst into plain view this week after Moonshot AI, a Chinese startup, unveiled an AI model with the technical prowess of many American ones — but which can be accessed at a fraction of the cost.

data.title: In the data center capital of the world, residents ask who the AI boom is helping
data.description: STERLING, Va. — When Hari Doue’s 9-year-old twins have tried to sleep in their suburban Virginia home, something has been keeping the boys awake: a “high-pitched whining” sound that wasn’t there before. The sound is generated by the data center that was built nearby.

data.title: New bipartisan bill takes aim at Chinese AI companies accused of copying American tech
data.description: A bipartisan group of lawmakers said they were introducing legislation Thursday aimed at protecting American artificial intelligence companies like OpenAI and Anthropic from Chinese competitors. The bill, called the Collaboration on Adversarial Threats and Security Risks Act, treats Chinese alleged copycats as a security threat to the U.S. and would create an exemption to antitrust laws so AI companies and industry groups can coordinate on a response, according to a summary of the legislation from the lawmakers’ offices. Antitrust laws generally limit intra-industry coordination on many subjects, on the theory that coordination may raise prices for consumers. The legislation is being introduced by Sens. Adam Schiff, D-Calif., and Jim Banks, R-Ind., and Reps. Bob Latta, R-Ohio, and George Whitesides, D-Calif. Lawmakers said the bill would allow AI companies and industry groups to share information related to certain security risks as long as they do so in good faith for the exclusive purpose of preventing, investigating or mitigating AI security risks. They could also coordinate — for the exclusive purpose of reducing AI security risks — by delaying or limiting the deployment of high-risk AI models, with required written notice before they take such actions, according to the summary of the legislation. The lawmakers said their antitrust exemption would include strict guardrails to keep the AI sector competitive. They said their legislation would also permit the attorney general to seek an injunction against organizations that abuse the exemption. It’s part of a wider effort by the U.S. government to protect the fast-growing AI industry from mounting Chinese competition. In recent weeks, several Chinese AI companies, including Moonshot and Z.ai, released new AI systems that appear to match leading American AI capabilities — a remarkable surge in Chinese AI performance. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent threatened in an interview Tuesday with Fox Business to sanction “overseas models” for “stealing from our great companies.” He repeated the threat Wednesday, saying on X that sanctions were “on the table.” Michael Kratsios, director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy, separately accused Moonshot of “stealing proprietary U.S. technology” Wednesday on X. Moonshot did not respond to a request for comment. Anthropic, Google and OpenAI are increasingly complaining about what’s known in the tech industry as “distillation”: submitting thousands or even millions of queries to an advanced AI model and processing its outputs in an attempt to improve an inferior AI model and create, in effect, a less expensive knockoff. China has rejected allegations that it is stealing American trade secrets, calling them an unfounded smear. “China has consistently attached great importance to the protection of intellectual property and is committed to safeguarding, in a fair and impartial manner, the lawful rights and interests of both domestic and foreign IP holders,” Liu Chang, a spokesperson for the Chinese Embassy in Washington, said in a statement Wednesday. “China’s development is driven by its own sustained efforts and has also benefited from mutually beneficial international cooperation,” he said. “Relevant individuals in the United States should respect the facts, discard prejudice, and stop smearing and discrediting China’s achievements in the development of its artificial intelligence industry.” The U.S. lawmakers said they believe that the threat is real and that AI firms need to be able to share information to counter it. “We cannot allow China, or any foreign adversary, to steal American technological advancements in AI, and then weaponize those advances to undermine our own national security,” Schiff, whose state is home to the leading U.S. AI labs, said in a statement. “As new AI innovations are developing faster than ever, it’s critical that Republicans and Democrats continue to work together to address potential misuse of AI development by hostile actors at home and abroad,” he said. Latta, chair of the House Energy and Commerce subcommittee on energy, said AI models are now critical infrastructure like the electric grid. “Protecting the American people means ensuring the systems they rely on every day, including our hospitals, water systems, and electric grid, remain safe, secure, and reliable,” he said in a statement. “Our adversaries are constantly looking for new ways to target our critical infrastructure, and we must make sure we are prepared to respond.” Antitrust laws limit coordination within an industry so businesses can’t agree to fix prices, divvy up territory or otherwise restrain trade. But Congress has on occasion expressly created exemptions when it has identified national security interests, as it did in 2015 when it passed an exception related to cybersecurity information-sharing. The Justice Department’s Antitrust Division also has the authority to grant temporary or limited exemptions for businesses to coordinate, a power it used in 2020 to allow vaccine makers to share information early in the Covid-19 pandemic. But within the tech industry, the value of distillation is hotly disputed, especially as customers face big bills from the leading U.S. AI labs. Arguing in favor of using low-price Chinese models, Nic Carter, a Miami-based venture capitalist, said in a widely shared post on X this week that American businesses and consumers “will benefit from hyperdeflation in the cost of digital cognition just like everyone else.” Another venture capitalist, Bill Gurley, said on X that AI firms should sue, rather than lobby the government, if they think their work is being unlawfully infringed upon. Some U.S. companies have been vocal in their defense of using foreign AI models. Hugging Face, which makes software used by AI labs, said last week that it turned to a Chinese model to successfully analyze a large-scale cyberattack after “frontier models” from the U.S. were no help. On Tuesday, OpenAI revealed in a blog post that one of its autonomous agents was behind the hack after it went rogue. Clement Delangue, the CEO of Hugging Face, praised distillation on X in April. “What people call ‘distillation’ is a super common practice (you use other models to benchmark your model, to evaluate your inputs or to add a little bit to your datasets) that in my opinion should be covered by fair use (just like using public data is), especially when the resulting models are open-source as it benefits all and helps break out monopolies that are strongly forming in AI!” he wrote. SpaceX CEO Elon Musk testified in April as part of his lawsuit against OpenAI that his AI researchers had used distillation techniques on OpenAI models to train his chatbot, Grok. Google offers a limited distillation service so enterprise customers can run their own smaller, cheaper versions of the Gemini chatbot.

data.title: The fight for the future of foldable phones is here
data.description: What’s the difference between a Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, Fold 8 and Flip 8? Two fold, one flips and all three unfold your wallet. (Sorry.) Making fun of Samsung’s product names is an old tradition. But what the company announced Wednesday is genuinely new and far more consequential than its usual summer foldable refresh. Apple is expected to unveil its first foldable iPhone in September, and RAM-ageddon continues to drive up all consumer electronics prices. I spoke with one of Samsung’s top executives about Apple’s expected entry into the market and Samsung’s own seven-year head start. But first, let’s break down the differences among the three devices, so you don’t have to run the product names through a Samsung-to-human translator. New shapes. Sure, there are small differences among the three phones, but shape is the biggest. Galaxy Z Fold 8: The “all-new” Fold opens like a children’s book or a passport, revealing a wider main landscape display with a 4:3 aspect ratio. Think classic Hollywood. It starts at $1,900. Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra: This one opens more like a tall pamphlet. It keeps the narrower, more vertical shape of Samsung’s previous Fold design. It also now has a ginormous battery. It starts at $2,100. Galaxy Z Flip 8: This one folds vertically, like the flip phones of the early 2000s. It starts at $1,200. All of these phones ship Aug. 7. The most interesting is the new Galaxy Z Fold 8 — or what Samsung probably should have just called the Fold Widescreen. (Seriously, drop the Z, Samsung. The Z stands for “zero reason to keep it.”) It opens into a wider, more tablet-like display that’s better for watching videos. Apple’s first foldable iPhone is rumored to have a similar shape. When I asked Drew Blackard, Samsung’s senior vice president of mobile product management, whether the Apple reports influenced the redesign, he said it was driven more by user experience. “Media consumption has really changed a lot since the first one we launched” in 2019, he said. Samsung is keeping the taller Fold 8 Ultra around as a more productivity-focused device. In other words: one Fold for watching, another Fold for working and a Flip for pretending it’s 2005 again. New prices. Blackard said the Fold 8 Ultra and the Flip 8 are each $100 more expensive than their predecessors. Both come with upgrades. The Ultra, for instance, has a larger battery — but he said rising RAM and storage costs were a “big driver” of the increases. “It’s the big challenge right now,” he said, though he suggested many U.S. buyers may not feel the full hit as carriers launch deals and other creative ways to hide it in the bill. New competitor. Looming over the entire announcement is Apple’s entry this fall. While Samsung has been betting for the last seven years on phones that expand into tablets, Apple will be making the same sell in whatever Apple way it devises. Asked about the competition, Blackard touted the lead Samsung has in improving the durability, design and functionality. He also speculated that “they’ll probably have one form factor, and we’re excited to be offering three now.” I don’t know, seems like only a matter of time until Apple gives us the iPhone Fold, Fold Pro and Fold Pro Max. Joanna Stern is NBC News’ chief tech analyst and founder of New Things, a new independent media company that covers consumer technology. A version of this article first appeared in her New Things newsletter.

data.title: China fires back at Trump administration claims that its AI companies steal U.S. tech
data.description: A Chinese government spokesperson fired back Wednesday at the Trump administration’s allegations that Chinese researchers are stealing American trade secrets about artificial intelligence.

data.title: Trump restricted China’s access to AI chips. The country is racing to build its own.
data.description: SHANGHAI — Chips were on the menu at China’s largest AI gathering over the weekend, as salespeople barked into microphones to tout advances in microscopic engineering and entourages trailed regional government officials marveling at brightly lit trade displays.

data.title: Critics of Christopher Nolan’s ‘The Odyssey’ turn to a new AI-generated film
data.description: As Christopher Nolan’s “The Odyssey” dominates the box office, a fully AI-generated adaptation of the same ancient epic has attracted attention from some right-wing audiences who are boosting it as a possible alternative to what they see as Hollywood’s “woke” creative direction.

data.title: OpenAI says AI models went rogue during testing, triggering ‘unprecedented’ breach at startup
data.description: OpenAI said on Tuesday that some of its AI models went rogue during a security test and triggered a hack that compromised the infrastructure of AI startup Hugging Face last week. In a blog post, OpenAI said it was testing the capabilities of some of its most advanced models in a controlled environment but that they managed to escape containment, reach the internet, and break into Hugging Face to try and satisfy their testing goal. The blog post said the breakout was “an unprecedented cyber incident, involving state-of-the-art cyber capabilities” and that the company was reinforcing its safeguards. Hugging Face, a platform used to host open-source large language models and datasets, caused a stir in the cybersecurity community when it said in a blog post last week that it had been the target of a hack that “was different from anything we had handled before” in that “it was driven, end to end, by an autonomous AI agent system.” OpenAI‘s disclosure that its advanced models were responsible for the breach, despite having placed them in what it described as “a highly isolated environment,” will likely intensify disquiet over the power and risk of frontier models.

data.title: Inside China’s biggest AI show: Robot police, dancing humanoids and a roof collapse
data.description: SHANGHAI — Hundreds of thousands of AI-curious visitors from China and abroad flocked to Shanghai for this weekend’s World Artificial Intelligence Conference, jostling to get a glimpse at China’s latest AI innovations.

data.title: AI is writing, acting and producing China’s minidramas. It’s shaking a $14 billion industry.
data.description: ZHENGZHOU, China — Chinese microdramas — known for hyperemotional stories about torrid love affairs, time-traveling revenge and explosive family secrets — are facing drama of their own as AI transforms an industry that is also rapidly expanding in the United States. Shot vertically for China’s mobile-first audience, microdramas — also called minidramas or short dramas — have captivated hundreds of millions of Chinese for years with fast-paced, addictive stories told in episodes that usually last between 60 and 90 seconds. But technology has upended the way they are written, produced and performed. “AI is changing everything,” Gao Qingge, a microdrama director based in Beijing, told NBC News in an interview. “Because AI can save a lot of money.” Out of about 100,000 microdramas released in the first three months of this year, about 95% were entirely produced by AI, according to the state-run People’s Daily newspaper, leaving many actors and crew members in the $14 billion industry in limbo. Though Chinese viewers are watching artificial intelligence microdramas in huge numbers, the shift has also created anxieties similar to those in Hollywood, where a full-length feature film starring controversial AI “actor” Tilly Norwood was criticized by a major actors union as “devaluing human artistry.” Just as microdramas blossomed in China before jumping to the U.S., where they are also produced and widely viewed, the way the industry embraces and restricts AI could have implications for American TV and film productions down the road. The changing mood is hard to miss in Zhengzhou, China’s microdrama capital, where many housing projects left unfinished amid a yearslong national property crisis have been transformed into filming bases. At Dazhi filming base outside the city of more than 12 million people, only four crews were filming late last month when NBC News visited the 200,000-square-foot production complex. There are more than 50 sets at the one-stop production venue, where it’s a short walk from a ballroom with grand chandeliers to a modern subway, a hospital, a karaoke room, a jail cell or the office of a wealthy CEO, allowing crews to shoot scores of episodes in a matter of days. Only a few months ago, as many as 15 crews could be shooting there at the same time, and sets were booked out weeks in advance, Wu Hongbin, the base’s manager, said. “Compared to last year, the current volume has basically been slashed by about 60% to 70%,” he said, in part because of the impact of AI. The shift has left those who depend on microdramas for employment at loose ends. Liu Yuan, 45, a microdrama actor who often plays the father of rich men, said he almost never used to have openings in his schedule. But many crews “ground to a halt” early this year, he said, when the popular microdrama platform Hongguo reportedly made policy changes that discouraged external production partners from making live-action series. “Most of them shifted to AI, so live-action minidramas became relatively scarce,” Liu said while filming at the complex, though he said work had picked up again more recently. Production companies are motivated by the idea of slashing what they spend on human actors, Gao said. “It saves five to eight times the amount of money if we use AI, compared to the live-action ones,” she said. AI also provides more flexibility than traditional TV productions, said Huang Dingxuan, a Beijing-based cinematographer who works with Gao on microdramas such as last year’s “Scapegoat Maid.” “If it’s raining outside but we have a sunny day [scripted], we have to wait, but with AI, if you want a sunny day, it’s going to be a sunny day,” he said. Anina Net, an American who has spent years acting in Chinese-directed microdramas for English-speaking audiences, said AI has been an “incredible tool” for series such as one she appeared in last year, “Daddy’s Got Girls and Guns.” “This particular director, Paddy Ma, he loves helicopters,” she said. “I’ve shot with him so many times and there’s always, you know, the girl getting out of the helicopter.” “Well, he wanted seven helicopters, but there’s no budget for seven helicopters,” said Net, who is based in Los Angeles. AI came to the rescue, she said, but it’s also a “double-edged sword.” “So many of the incredibly professional people that I know that were working every day are back home doing nothing because there’s no more live-action shootings,” Net said. Net says she has a “no AI” clause in her contracts for her own image, as “I cannot risk that someone would make me say something that I absolutely never said or do things that I never did.” Some companies are trying to get ahead of those concerns, with mixed results. The Chinese streaming giant iQIYI faced backlash in April after unveiling an AI performer database that it said would allow actors to authorize the creation of digital doubles for future films and TV shows. Though iQIYI said the actors in its database would have control over how their images were used, Chinese actors in other cases have complained about their faces and voices being cloned and used in ways they didn’t agree to. In April, Hongguo removed a popular AI-generated series, “Peach Blossom Hairpin,” and penalized its production company after accusations that it had used online users’ likenesses without their consent. Chinese authorities have begun cracking down on such abuses, and since last year China has required that all AI-generated content be clearly labeled. Though some U.S. states have transparency laws that require AI-generated content to be labeled, lawmakers in Congress are still debating federal legislation. Despite the growing influence of AI, some people in the industry remain optimistic. Wu said he still believes that human productions will bounce back because viewers like to watch real people. “When I watch the AI characters, I have the ‘uncanny valley’ feeling,” one user on Chinese social media platform RedNote commented last month, referring to the unsettled feeling that people can experience when looking at humanoid objects. Ma Shuchang, a college student in Zhengzhou who acts in microdramas in her free time, has noticed the slowdown in work but says she still enjoys AI-generated series. “Watching AI dramas is great because they have a lot of special effects that real-life dramas just can’t achieve,” she said, adding that the two can “complement each other.” “Moving forward, high-quality productions with meaningful content will definitely be the ones that win people over,” Ma said. Janis Mackey Frayer, Dawn Liu and Erin Tan reported from Zhengzhou, and Larissa Gao from London.
